data.title: Tabuk Agricultural Development Posts Narrower Q1 Loss; Revenue Up
data.description:
Tabuk Agricultural Development Co. (SASE:6040) on Thursday posted a smaller loss for the first quarter while revenue jumped year over year.

Net loss for the three months ended March 31 was 7.1 million Saudi riyals, compared with 9.3 million riyals earlier. Loss per share moved to 0.18 riyal from 0.23 riyal.

The Saudi Arabia-listed agricultural company's revenue was 13.1 million riyals, compared with 7.9 million riyals a year ago.

data.title: Swiss Re Posts Profit Beat Despite Softer Revenue
data.description:
By Elena Vardon

Swiss Re reported a larger-than-expected increase in first-quarter net profit despite a softer topline as the group was able to absorb the impact of the wildfires that ravaged California in January.

The Zurich-based reinsurer on Friday posted a net profit of $1.275 billion for the three months ended March 31, representing a 16% on-year increase, which surpassed a $938 million estimate taken from a company-compiled consensus.

Resilient underwriting results as well as favorable investment returns and tax impacts helped beat expectations. Revenue fell 11% compared with its result from a year prior, which was boosted by a one-off from accounting changes and the end of an insurance deal in its life unit, it said. Insurance revenue came in at $10.405 billion, missing views of $11.98 billion.

Swiss Re said that large natural-catastrophe claims in its property-and-casualty segment amounted to $570 million and was mainly related to the fires in Los Angeles at the start of the quarter, while man-made losses totalled $140 million.

Chief Executive Andreas Berger said that the group is confident in its 2025 targets despite the challenging environment.

Its Swiss solvency test ratio stood at 254% as of April 1.

Write to Elena Vardon at elena.vardon@wsj.com

data.title: REG-RUBIS: Request to add resolutions to the agenda of the general meeting on 12 june 2025, by Compagnie Nationale de Navigation
data.description:
Paris, 16 May 2025, 7:45am

Rubis announces that on 15 May 2025, it received a request from Compagnie Nationale de Navigation to add two draft resolutions to the agenda. These resolutions pertain to the appointment of Mr. Patrick Molis and Mrs. Anne Lauvergeon as members of the Supervisory Board for a term of three years.

The Management Board welcomes the candidacies of Mr. Patrick Molis and Mrs. Anne Lauvergeon, initiated by Compagnie Nationale de Navigation, a shareholder holding more than 9% of Rubis' share capital. Mr. Patrick Molis has expressed, during various exchanges with the Company, his willingness to engage in a constructive dialogue.

The Supervisory Board of Rubis will meet on 21 May 2025 to review these requests and will issue an opinion on them.

These additional requests will be available on the Rubis website and will be included in the agenda under the conditions provided by law.

Media Relations Contact
RUBIS – Communication Department RUBIS – Legal Department
Email : presse@rubis.fr Tel. : + 33 (0)1 44 17 95 95

Attachment

  • RUBIS: Request to add resolutions to the agenda of the general meeting on 12 june 2025, by Compagnie Nationale de Navigation

data.title: Manila Electric Likely Lacks Further Rerating Catalysts — Market Talk
data.description: Manila Electric appears to lack any further rerating catalysts, SB Equities' Katrine Eunice Dolatre says in a research report. Its share price has risen 18% year-to-date through May 14, thanks to recent announcements including franchise renewal, the analyst notes. Also, its share price looks a bit overvalued based on the current price-to-earnings and price-to-book-value ratios, the analyst says. Hence, the brokerage downgrades the stock's rating to hold from add. However, given SB Equities makes no adjustments to its current assumptions about the Philippine electric distribution utility, the brokerage maintains the target price at PHP562.80. Shares are 1.3% lower at PHP562.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: German 10-Year Bund Yield Seen Drifting Higher in Coming Months — Market Talk
data.description: ​​The German 10-year Bund yield is expected move higher on a six- to 12-month horizon, to be driven by rising state expenditures, DZ Bank Research's Birgit Henseler says in a note. "The planned state investment programs as well as higher defense expenditures in Germany will probably lead to rising new debt," the analyst says. The rising supply of government bonds as well as an economic pickup could lead to higher 10-year Bund yields, she says. DZ Bank Research expects the 10-year Bund yield at 2.70% in six months, to 2.85% by year-end and to 3.00% in 12 months, the analyst says. The 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.624% on Thursday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Australia's Hastings Technology rises on Wyloo deal, set for best session since April
data.description:
** Shares of Hastings Technology Metals rise 6.3% to A$0.34, set for their strongest trading session since April 17, if gains sustain

** The rare-earths developer executes definitive agreement with Australian billionaire Andrew Forrest's Wyloo first announced in February

** Hastings to sell 60% interest in Yangibana rare-earths and niobium project in Western Australia to Wyloo

data.title: Malaysia's GDP Expands Softly at 4.4% in Q1
data.description:
Malaysia's economy expanded 4.4% in the first quarter, softer than the 4.9% growth in the preceding quarter, according to data released by the country's Department of Statistics on Friday.

On a seasonally adjusted basis, the economy edged up 0.7%, reversing a 0.2% decline in the fourth quarter of 2024.

On the supply side, the Services sector remained the primary driver of economic growth during the quarter, with all other sectors showing positive growth except for Mining and Quarrying.

Meanwhile, on the demand side, the growth was largely fueled by Private Final Consumption Expenditure and Gross Fixed Capital Formation, the data showed.

data.title: Malaysia's Current Account Books MYR16.7 Billion Surplus in Q1
data.description:
Malaysia's current account balance recorded a surplus of 16.7 billion ringgit in the first quarter, according to data released by the country's Department of Statistics on Friday.

The surplus was supported by net exports of goods and a smaller deficit in secondary income.

Meanwhile, the financial account registered a larger net outflow of 20.3 billion ringgit, versus 9.3 billion ringgit in the previous quarter, primarily due to movements in portfolio investments.

International reserves stood at 520.7 billion ringgit as at the end of the first quarter, slightly better than 520.1 billion ringgit in the preceding quarter.

Foreign direct investment posted a lower net inflow of 15.6 billion ringgit, compared with 18.7 billion ringgit in the previous quarter, the data showed.

data.title: Rubis Gets Request From Compagnie Nationale De Navigation to Add Resolutions to AGM Agenda
French Unemployment Rate Climbs to 7.4% in Q1
data.description:
France's unemployment rate came in at 7.4% in the first quarter, up from 7.3% in the prior three-month period, the statistics agency Insee said Friday.

The latest reading is consistent with the consensus estimate for the quarter.

data.title: Eni: Buyback to Be Executed by April 2026
Eni: Buyback Amount May Be Increased Up to a Maximum of EUR3.5B
Dollar tracks Treasury yields lower on benign US data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Dollar eases but on track for slight weekly gain
  • South Korean won still choppy after sharp rises this week
  • Japan's economy shrinks as U.S. tariff hit looms

The dollar fell in tandem with U.S. Treasury yields on Friday after downside surprises on U.S. economic data this week cemented bets of more Federal Reserve rate cuts this year.

The week started out with a mix of market tailwinds headlined by a U.S.-China trade truce which propelled the dollar higher, though the euphoria soon fizzled out and left currencies trading sideways.

Most of the action in the foreign exchange market came from the dollar's moves against the South Korean won, where it fell sharply for a second straight day on news that Washington and Seoul discussed the dollar/won market earlier this month.

The moves were reminiscent of a similar episode in the Taiwan dollar earlier this month.

The dollar last traded 0.4% lower at 1,390 won .

"Speculation is once again mounting that President Trump favours a weaker dollar, potentially pressuring other governments to allow their currencies to appreciate in trade negotiations," said George Vessey, lead FX and macro strategist at Convera.

"Asian currency weakness against the dollar has long been seen as an advantage for regional exporters, a stance the administration has sought to challenge."

In the broader market, the dollar was struggling to regain its footing after an overnight slide following data which showed U.S. producer prices unexpectedly fell in April.

The PPI figures came on the heels of a tame consumer price reading earlier in the week, cementing bets that the Fed is likely to cut rates at least twice this year.

The euro was up 0.26% to $1.2130 while sterling rose 0.14% to $1.3325.

Against a basket of currencies , the dollar fell 0.2% to 100.57, though was on track for a slight weekly gain thanks to its sharp 1.3% rise on Monday.

Markets are now pricing in roughly 57 basis points worth of Fed cuts by December following Thursday's data, up from 49 bps previously. (0#USDIRPR)

The benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury yield extended its 7 bps drop from overnight and was last a touch lower at 4.4217%. The two-year yield (US2YT=RR) fell to 3.9467%.

In a closely watched speech on Thursday, Fed Chair Jerome Powell said policymakers feel they need to reconsider the key elements around both jobs and inflation in their current approach to monetary policy.

"Chair Powell said that the FOMC will be placing more weight on the inflation outlook than on employment when setting monetary policy following a monetary policy framework review. This suggests a potentially higher hurdle to Fed cuts if inflation risks remain to the upside," said Kristina Clifton, senior currency strategist at Commonwealth Bank of Australia.

"We forecast three FOMC interest rate cuts this year. But the risks lie towards less cuts if inflation picks up."

Elsewhere, the dollar was down 0.33% against the yen at 145.30 , in line with the broader market, despite Friday's downbeat GDP data in Japan and dovish remarks from a Bank of Japan policymaker.

"The data...adds to the stress on optimism as Japan braces for the impact of tariffs. We expect a reasonable deal with the U.S. in the coming months which should alleviate the impact," said Krishna Bhimavarapu, APAC economist at State Street Global Advisors.

"All this will mean that the Bank of Japan will comfortably sit on the sidelines till certainty emerges as we expect just one hike this year, perhaps in Q4."

Investors also had their eye on potential talks between Tokyo and Washington next week, where Japanese Finance Minister Katsunobu Kato said he would seek to discuss foreign exchange issues with U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

In Australia, the Aussie rose 0.39% to $0.6430, while the New Zealand dollar gained 0.6% to $0.5910.

data.title: Techstep Extends Exclusive Mobile Devices Deal with Norwegian Health Sector
data.description:
Mobile and circular technology company Techstep said Friday its frame agreement with Sykehusinnkjøp HF was extended until July 2027.

The national procurement agency for Norway's specialist healthcare service granted Techstep exclusive rights to deliver mobile devices and services to all public hospitals in Norway. The company will also manage and recycle the mobile fleet for the hospitals.

Additionally, Techstep secured an agreement with Sykehuspartner HF to equip healthcare professionals in the Sør-Øst region, including at Oslo Universitetssykehus HF and Vestre Viken HF, with clinical role-based mobile devices in the fall season, aiming to improve patient care.

data.title: Open House Group's Profit Down 10% in Fiscal H1
data.description:
Open House Group recorded a 10% decline in attributable profit for the six months ended March 31 to 46.6 billion yen from 51.9 billion yen during the year-ago period, a Thursday filing on the Tokyo bourse said.

The life insurance company's EPS was 400.33 yen per share in the half year, down from 432.37 yen per share in the corresponding period last year.

Net sales for the fiscal H1 rose by 6.7% year on year to 643.4 billion yen from 602.8 billion yen.

The company declared an interim dividend of 84 yen per share.

For the fiscal year ending Sept. 30, the firm expects an attributable profit of 100 billion yen, basic earnings of 860.35 yen per share, and net sales of 1.310 trillion yen.

Open House, also expects to pay a total dividend of 178.00 yen per share for the current fiscal year.

data.title: Yomiuri: Japan's Chief Tariff Negotiator Aims to Visit U.S. Next Week
data.description:
By Masaomi Shimosato

Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

The Japanese government is making arrangements to send economic revitalization minister Ryosei Akazawa to the United States as early as the latter part of next week for a third round of negotiations over U.S. tariff policy.

The schedule will be finalized after working-level discussions with Washington, according to sources.

The focal point of this round will be whether the two sides can agree on the scope of negotiations. Japan is demanding that all tariffs newly introduced by the administration of U.S. President Donald Trump be lifted, while the United States insists that the talks should only cover "reciprocal tariffs."

Japan and the United States held their second round of negotiations on May 1. The two sides discussed topics such as trade expansion, reviewing non-tariff barriers and cooperation on economic security. They agreed to conduct intensive ministerial-level talks from mid-May, and they also launched working-level discussions.

In addition to the reciprocal tariffs, Japan plans to ask the United States to lift its tariffs on cars, auto parts and steel and aluminum products in the next round of negotiations. Aiming to obtain concessions from Washington, Japan has proposed a plan to increase imports of corn and soybeans so that the U.S. trade deficit will be reduced, and offered a proposal to cooperate in the shipbuilding sector.

The Japanese government on Thursday held a second meeting of its cross-sectoral task force on the U.S. tariffs. "We will make every effort to find the middle ground with the United States," Akazawa told reporters after the meeting. "We will protect what needs to be protected and say what needs to be said."

A plan has surfaced within the government to set a target of reaching an agreement with Washington by around the time of the next Group of Seven summit, to be held in June.

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal were not involved in the creation of this content.

data.title: New Zealand Shares Fall as US-China Trade Truce Rally Ends, Fletcher Building CEO Steps Down
data.description:
New Zealand shares fell on Friday as most Asian indexes ended their rally caused by the US-China trade truce earlier this week.

The S&P/NZX 50 Index fell 0.73% or 94.03 points to close at 12,786.79.

"The markets confront a weekend with less risk of carrying open positions than last, with no major trade talks or significant risks on the calendar," said Kyle Rodda, senior analyst at Capital.com, as quoted by Reuters.

Closer to home, New Zealand has decided to allocate higher budget funds to give rebates to foreign studios for making movies in the country as a response to Trump imposing 100% tariffs on films made outside the US, Reuters said in a separate report.

"We are sending a clear message to the world: New Zealand is the best place in the world to make movies. Bring your productions here to take advantage of our talent and locations," said Finance Minister Nicola Willis, as quoted by Reuters.

In domestic news, New Zealand's manufacturing sector expanded for a fourth month in a row in April as new orders returned to growth, according to a Friday report by BusinessNZ. The BusinessNZ Performance of Manufacturing Index rose to 53.9 in April from 53.2 in March.

In corporate news, Fletcher Building said Gareth O'Reilly, chief executive of its Australian division, will step down as the company conducts a broader strategic restructuring.

Investore Property reported Friday that it returned to profit in the fiscal year ended March 31 with earnings of NZ$0.1024 per diluted share, compared with a loss of NZ$0.1817 per diluted share a year earlier.

data.title: Singtel Raises $1.54 Billion From Airtel Stake Sale
data.description:
(04:40 GMT) *Singtel: Subsidiary Pastel Sold 71M Bharti Airtel Shares for About S$2B
(04:41 GMT) *Singtel: Shares Represent About 1.2% of Bharti Airtel's Total Number of Equity Shares
(05:39 GMT) Singtel Raises $1.54 Billion From Airtel Stake Sale

By Kimberley Kao

Singapore Telecommunications has raised US$1.54 billion from selling a portion of its stake in Indian telecommunications company Bharti Airtel, part of the telecom company's active capital-management efforts to optimize its portfolio.

Singtel said Friday that the divestment of 2.0 billion Singapore dollars, equivalent to US$1.54 billion, will result in an estimated S$1.4 billion gain. The sale was executed via a private placement to international and Indian institutional investors, including existing Bharti Airtel shareholders. Singtel said the placement was well oversubscribed.

After the sale, Singtel will hold a 28.3% stake in Bharti Airtel, valued at about S$48 billion.

"The divestment underscores Singtel's commitment to disciplined capital allocation and sustained value realization for shareholders," said Group Chief Financial Officer Arthur Lang.

In 2022 and 2024, Singtel raised a total of about S$3.5 billion from the progressive sale of Airtel shares.

The funds have helped support Singtel's 5G deployment, expand its digital infrastructure and retain sustainable shareholder distributions, it said.

Singtel has been a strategic investor in New Delhi-based Airtel for more than 20 years.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: Cartier owner Richemont sales up 7% as jewellery shines
data.description:
Cartier owner Richemont on Friday reported a slightly better-than-expected 7% rise in quarterly sales as wealthy shoppers shrugged off economic uncertainty and continued to splash out on luxury jewellery.

The Swiss-based company, which also owns jewellery brand Van Cleef & Arpels and watch labels Piaget and Jaeger-LeCoultre, said sales in its fourth quarter to end-March rose to 5.17 billion euros ($5.80 billion), a 7% rise in constant currencies.

That is slightly more than the 6% expected, according to a Visible Alpha consensus cited by HSBC and slightly slower than the 10% growth rate in the third quarter.

($1 = 0.8921 euros)

data.title: Insurer Aegon plans share buyback after soft 1st quarter results
data.description:
Aegon plans to run a new 200 million euro ($224 million) share buyback, the Dutch insurer said on Friday, after unfavourable mortality in its main U.S. market hit capital generation in the first quarter.

The new buyback would be carried out in the second half of 2025 and it would supplement the ongoing 150 million euro programme that was 68% completed by March 31, the company said.

Its operating capital generation rose slightly to 267 million euros in the first quarter, but missed the median consensus of 275 million euros from analysts polled by Aegon.

"In the first quarter of 2025, we continued to make progress in transforming our businesses," CEO Lard Friese said in a statement. "While the macroeconomic environment is uncertain, we expect to meet our 2025 financial targets."

Aegon expects to reach operating capital generation of around 1.2 billion euros this year.

Its Solvency II ratio, representing an insurer's ability to cover its liabilities, was 189% in the quarter, compared to analysts' forecast of 190%.

($1 = 0.8922 euros)

data.title: Richemont: Group Performance Was Robust Overall
Iceland completes Islandsbanki stake sale for over $700 million
data.description:
The Icelandic government said late on Thursday it had completed the sale of its 45.2% stake in Islandsbanki for 90.58 billion Icelandic crowns ($702.66 million), divesting a holding that was a legacy of the 2008 financial crisis.

Iceland on Tuesday launched the sale of a 20% stake in the lender in a process that prioritised domestic retail investors, and later expanded the offering to include its entire remaining stake in the bank due to high demand.

"The offering received significant institutional and substantial local demand, with considerable interest from both retail and institutional investors," the finance ministry said in a statement.

Shares were offered at a fixed price of 106.56 Icelandic crowns - or a 5% discount over the average price of the 15 trading days ahead of the sale launch - to retail investors.

Investor appetite substantially exceeded available shares, with total bids reaching 190 billion crowns, more than double the value of shares ultimately sold, the finance ministry.

The Icelandic government had acquired 75% of the bank during the 2008 financial crisis, which roiled the country's banking sector and plunged the economy into recession.

The government had sold part of its Islandsbanki stake in 2022.

($1 = 128.9100 Icelandic Crowns)
